questions.question: Which of the following is the correct definition of the half-life of a radioactive isotope?
options: The time taken for half the nuclei of an isotope in a sample to decay
The time taken for all the nuclei in a sample to decay by half their mass
Half the time it takes for a radioactive sample to become completely safe
The time taken for the proton number of a nucleus to be halved
questions.explanation:
Half-life is defined as the time taken for half the nuclei of a radioactive isotope in a sample to decay. It is a constant characteristic for a specific isotope.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A radioactive sample has a half-life of $10\text{ minutes}$. If the initial count rate is $800\text{ counts/s}$, what will the count rate be after $10\text{ minutes}$?
questions.explanation:
By definition, the count rate falls by exactly half during one half-life period. $800 / 2 = 400$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which unit is commonly used to express the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: Any unit of time (e.g., seconds, hours, or years)
questions.explanation:
Half-life is a measure of time duration. Depending on the isotope, it can range from $\frac{tions}{}$ of a second to billions of years.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: What happens to the half-life of a radioactive isotope as the sample gets smaller over time?
options: It decreases because there are fewer atoms
It increases as the remaining atoms become more stable
questions.explanation:
The half-life is a constant property of a particular radioactive isotope and does not change regardless of the sample size or time elapsed.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: If a sample has a very 'long' half-life, what does this imply about its rate of decay?
options: The sample does not emit any radiation
questions.explanation:
A long half-life means it takes a great deal of time for the activity to halve, indicating a slow rate of decay.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
